items.q: What are the 4 Cs of food hygiene?
items.correct: Cleaning, Cooking, Chilling, and avoiding Cross-contamination.
items.why: Students guess at the Cs. They specifically address the four food-safety risks: dirty surfaces, undercooked food, warm storage, and pathogens transferring between foods (raw to ready-to-eat).

items.q: On a traffic-light label, what does a red light indicate?
items.correct: High amounts of that nutrient per 100g - eat less often.
items.misconception: The food is dangerous and should never be eaten.
items.why: Students treat red as 'forbidden'. Traffic lights are a guidance system - red = high, eat less often or in small portions. Many foods (cheese, olive oil) are red on certain nutrients but fine in moderation.

items.correct: Acidic conditions (low pH) prevent most bacteria from growing.
items.misconception: The vinegar kills the food cells.
items.why: Students think pickling poisons bacteria. It works by creating an environment INHOSPITABLE to most microbes - low pH stops them growing, even if they're present.

items.q: Per gram, which provides more energy: fat or sugar?
items.correct: Fat - 9 kcal per gram, more than double the roughly 4 kcal per gram from sugar.
items.misconception: Sugar - sugary foods are the most fattening so sugar must have the most energy per gram.
items.random_wrong: They provide exactly the same energy per gram.
items.why: Students equate 'sugary' with 'most calorific'. Sugar (a carbohydrate) gives about 4 kcal/g; fat gives 9 kcal/g - the most energy-dense nutrient. That is why high-fat foods are so calorie-dense even in small portions.

items.q: Do carbohydrates themselves make you gain weight?
items.correct: No - weight gain happens when total energy intake from any nutrient exceeds energy used.
items.misconception: Yes - eating any carbohydrates causes fat to be stored.
items.random_wrong: No - only protein can cause weight gain.
items.why: Students blame one nutrient. The Eatwell Guide recommends starchy carbohydrates make up over a third of food eaten. It is overall energy balance that matters: surplus energy from carbohydrate, fat OR protein is stored as body fat.
It's Fat. “Carbohydrate” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: 30 g. “10 g” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The real figure is 4 kcal. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
